speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three. Today is January 2, 1997. I'm interviewing Shirley Warman. We were talking about, your ride from Lutsk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you were saying that there was a time on the train that you found out that you were not going to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you learned that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just think I hope I survive. I don't know how far we're going. People started to cry. And I said, look, crying will not help us. Let's see what we could do. But there was nothing else to do. We couldn't-- we-- we couldn't run away. And that there was no place where to run. That's it. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, weeks. I don't even remember. Weeks. We left, Lutsk was warm. And we came-- we arrived to Siberia. It was-- it was the fall already. You felt chilly. Then they-- they took-- they took us out from these cattle trains, and they put us on trucks. It was open trucks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we start to look, and we saw the forest, and it was like to the sky. I said, my god, what are we going to do here? I don't see no people, no houses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just forest and water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- we were quiet. We didn't talk anything, just quiet, till we arrived in the place. It was raining. It was at night. It was cold already. We didn't have anywhere to go to sleep. Till the two cars, the Russian people were there. I don't know how long they were there. This was, I think, when Stalin was-- I don't know. The rich people, they send away to Siberia. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of them were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of them. A lot of them. It was night. We didn't-- we didn't know what's what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Siberia was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very deep Siberia. We didn't see no houses, we didn't see no people. There was farms, but I don't know. We didn't see them. People said there were a lot of farms there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were other people living in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Russian people, yes. And they took us in. We didn't have anywhere to sleep. And they brought-- how you call it-- straw. You know, like you give the cows to eat. And then we put it, and there-- there was two layers, top from wood, and the bottom. And we put this, and we started to sleep like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who helped you with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We helped ourselves. Nobody helped us anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody supervise this? Any Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Russian people, yes, they supervised this.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russian people on the train with you, the soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The guards. No, no guards. These people were the-- they were over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The people who were already there.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They-- they-- they took them out. A lot of them, they were left over. Left. You know? They should supervise us. Where they went, I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did they leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away when we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was left then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a lot of them left, and they started to tell us what to do. In no time, we went to work. They told us, if you're not going to work, you're not going to get food.

speaker: interviewer
text: The type of housing that you described, was it an enclosed area or open?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an open-- open. What that was-- you know, the-- they were like shacks, from wood made. One near the others, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in a shack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was mine-- in my little room-- how many was it? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 8 Or 10, I don't remember. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they separate women's shacks, or men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was man and woman. There was no separate things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the sleeping arrangements, how did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody-- we didn't fight. Everybody had a place. I want to sleep here. Go ahead, sleep here. Sleep on the top, sleep  on the bottom. There was no-- there was no-- no-- no discussions about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, families, they want to sleep together. I mean, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same type of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the same type. They're-- nobody has-- no family has a separate, like, room, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in your shack, there were families living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Just people without-- you know, no families. Single people. I was lucky, we had-- we were just women.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They died. I saw-- I saw them here in America. They're dead, every single one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sanitary conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no place where to go wash up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was cold. There was a-- there was a-- like a-- a stove made in the-- how do you call it? Not cement. With sand, you know, like-- I don't know how you call this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a-- a stove made. And there was two burners, but you've got to put wood to make it to-- to burn. And-- and they gave us not-- not pots. You know when-- how do you call it? There's cans, from big cans, like from jam or other stuff-- cans, we get-- they give us cans.

speaker: interviewee
text: We should cook in these cans, a bowl of water, and the wooden spoons. They did. And everybody, you know, there was a big kitchen. They cooked for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: They pick people from these kind of people. There was Russian too, but you know, they're supervisors. And they took the older people there. The young people they put in the-- in the forest to cut the trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the older people stayed and did the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cooking. They were cooking for us. You know, if you're lucky, you were lucky, you stay with your can. They put from the bottom, you got a little bit thicker food, you know, soup. And if they put in the top, you got the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you said that the sanitary conditions were bad--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- you know. We boiled water. There was a-- like a veil. You gotta-- you know, turn-- I don't know how to say it, with a big pail from wood. And in the winter, you couldn't do it. But everything was frozen. We cut, you know, the bread with a hack, you know, the ice. And we washed ourselves, just like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you cover yourself in the-- in the winter, when you slept?

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us-- you know. A lot of people brought with us, but I didn't have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was blankets. The-- the other Russians left with blankets here. No pillows, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there anything like lice or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Every one of us had lice, every single one of us. We wash our clothes, you know. We boiled water. And they gave us big pails. And we boiled the clothes, they should-- the lice shouldn't-- you know, that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about bathrooms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside you go. There was no bathrooms. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no toilet paper. Cut a piece of-- you know, how you call, a leaf. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the situation with food?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you work, you got-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 200 grams. I-- I don't know how many it is, 200 grams-- grams of bread you got every day. As when-- this was [INAUDIBLE]. You know? They made the bread. The bread was so hard. And they give you some-- a little bit of sugar, and they give you-- that's all. Then was the-- the kitchen. You went in for soup. They give you the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who distributed the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. The Russians. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody get special treatment for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You get-- I don't know. I-- I couldn't say. I don't want to talk about it, this. If you work, over there, you say [INAUDIBLE]. You know, you worked very hard, you got some more things. I was the lucky one. I worked very hard. I was on the list on the wall, and I got privileges. I got some more bread. I got a little bit more sugar. I got a little powder milk. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was on a daily basis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I had a piece of soap.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I was standing with a cudgel and boiled water for the workers who were working in the forest, to cut the trees, and to take out the trees, you know, the-- the wood, to put them under water, like straps. Then the supervisor passed by with a-- on a horse. And he asked me what I'm doing here. I said, I'm staying here, and I boiled water for the workers for lunch, they should have the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said a cudgel, what-- what do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like a big container. You boiled water. Very big, and underneath, you put the branches from the-- from the-- from the trees. And they boiled the water. You gotta go to the-- there was a well with two pails to bring the water. It was about 10 pails, you know, go into this water. And everybody came with the can. And you gotta keep-- you gotta-- this can, you've got to save. If not, you're not getting another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: The can that they gave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And the wooden spoon. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] So he said, I can't stay here. He's going to put on all the men. And he's going to put me in the forest. I should stay with somebody and cut the trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said I'm too young for this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the supervisor, a Russian. And he took me off. He put a man, a Jewish man, an older guy. He has five children. And we start to cut the trees. And we-- and when you-- you know, when you cut the tree, and then when you sawed it, and was start to be a wind, the tree is supposed to fall this way, and it turned, and  it fell the other way, and it killed the guy on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We. We did-- nobody did it. We cut the tree.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but who killed this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tree fell on this side, and the tree fell on the-- on the guy, and he was dead. We buried him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? How did you bury somebody there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very simple. You dig a-- a-- a-- a hole, you put him in, and you put the-- the dirt on him, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right where he died, or was his body--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was a place. Was a lot of people died over there. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish people who died, and they had families, were there any burial--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, who did-- who knew this? Nobody. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He took my place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of tools did they give you to cut down the trees?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. A-- a saw. Where you saw-- two people have to stay and saw. And like you say, a hack, you know, when you cut something. I don't know how do you say it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a wooden handle and a sharp. And you-- before you-- before you cut a little bit the tree. And then you've got to saw. If not, you can't put the saw in. And then there's another-- other people stay and cut the tree, the branches with a little hack, with a little thing, and they cut off all the branches. And there's people with horses and with rope or chains. And they put the trees with the chains to the horse, and they took him on some-- on a place. Someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where they took them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, to the-- there was a big [INAUDIBLE]. And they put there, it was laid like slabs of wood, and they put it, and this was flown away. I don't know where that this went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? When was-- when we went it was dark, when we came home, it was dark. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was where you worked from where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far. Everything was in one place. Not far. But in the summertime, it was better. But the ants ate us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ants?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ants. The mosquitoes. Everybody was wearing a little bit, you know, the muslin over your face. If not, they eat you up till blood. And the winter is the supervisors went first and skied. And they put-- you know, we-- where they make, like, a way, and we went after him. If not, the snow was still here, we couldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened on-- on the job? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Were there supervisors there that punished people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They was very nice of us. And they made a big fire. We should stay by the fire when it was very, very cold. And everything was frozen. They-- they-- we had the material shoes, you know, like heavy. And-- and you know, when you go in the snow, it gets frozen. And we stay by the fire. They said, I'm going to look over at the supervisors, the other ones. You know, the top of him. But if I'm going to see him arriving, I'm going to make you-- you have to go right away to work. They were very nice to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the supervisors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian. Russian. They were-- they were sending them. They was very rich. You know, they was--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were prisoners too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoners, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the guards at work? Were there Russian guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Russian guards. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nice to us. They were very nice to us. We didn't harm them, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded the camp? Who-- who--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no guarding, nothing. What-- you had no place where to go. A lot of my friends, young people, three boys, they came from a very, very rich family. They couldn't take this. They start to run, and the bears ate them up. We never find them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know it was the bears?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing else. People was talking. I don't know. They were just talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to escape and was caught by the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Myself and another boy. And we were caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But we said, we're not-- we're-- we're not running away. We just went to look for something in the farms for potatoes. So they said, OK, this time we'll let you-- let you go. And they said, you have no place where to run here. There's no way you could survive. That's-- they let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three hours. But there was no place to go. We saw farms. We stole a lot of potatoes. We see if nobody looks. And cabbage. They took away from us. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was dogs, a lot of dogs. They start to bark. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they send the dogs after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No. We said, we go-- we just went to the farms to get some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get back to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking. They took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing happened. Nothing. We cried, and that's it. The other people was said, in a way, we're glad you're back. But we were very happy if you would make it. But there was no way you could make that. There was no way. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- when you said, you cried, what were you thinking at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think anything. Just crying, and I went to sleep. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else happened in the workplace?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing happened. Just you know, when-- and the-- we went out, and there was-- in the summertime, it was better. We went out, and we saw each other. We talked. And everybody said, we're going to survive, and it's going to be OK. And that's all. But-- that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any religious observances there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When-- we've all forgot about this, but a lot of people knew, remembered. There was Rosh Hashana, Yom Kippur. And we were fasting, and we didn't go out to work. And they make it go to work. And who didn't go out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took me in for three days to jail, without food-- with no-- it was like-- like a hole with no windows. There was a pail if you have to make. And for three days and three nights, then when I-- when they took me out, I couldn't see anything. It was very dark. And our day, it was a bright light. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were other people not going to work on the holidays too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't know what happened to them. Nobody talks. Nobody said anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Russians allow any Jewish services?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, no. No. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they done sort of underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Everybody was afraid. You were afraid to open your mouth, even with whom you slept. You know, came a time which everybody was for themselves. Thinking. What are they thinking? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to anybody? Did you get close to anybody there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just talking. That's it. Passing time. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get sick there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I got sick in the forest. And they took me home. And I was laying 10 days. Nobody showed up. I could have died. In a hospital, they took me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How close to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp. It was no-- it was not far. And the doctor came in. And I was sleeping all the time. And I was-- I don't know. And after 10 days, I went back to work. I was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I got fever. A lot of-- I had fever. And I had malaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was malaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was malaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they treat you? Did you get medical care?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. Yes. They gave me [INAUDIBLE]. That's-- yeah, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they feed you there. They feed you, and they give you-- you know, you go wash up. You-- but I was very sick. I couldn't get down from bed. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that people die there.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people died with starvation. Couldn't work in the forest. By starvation, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who buried them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bury them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, what else do you do? It was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: They died in your presence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in my presence. But we all went. But nobody paid attention, even. Everybody said, today him, tomorrow me. That's it. This was life. Nobody expected anything. There was not a big deal. One woman said, he's better off. He's dead already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they bury the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a place over there. There was a big place. You know? And we buried them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you punished any other times?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was working in the woods, in the forest. And I was cutting off the branches from the trees, and I took a bunch of branches to bring to the barracks to make myself warm. They caught me, and they punished me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say they caught you, who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean the guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: He slapped my face. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I begged him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, please, I'm very cold. And it's-- it's-- it's very cold. We've got to get something. Why don't you be human like-- we're not human anymore. We don't know if we are human. And he started to cry. He let me go home with the branches. I came home, and everybody was so happy we were warm. We boiled water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you punished any other time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was punished once-- I couldn't-- I couldn't cut anymore the trees. My arm was very bad. So he kicked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Somebody else. He kicked me. But I didn't-- I didn't beg. And I didn't say anything. I didn't even cry. I didn't have no tears. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk about the weather there being very cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the weather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Siberia at that time, all the 20 hours day or 20 hours night. In the summer, it's very hot, and the mosquitoes eat you up. And there was bugs. But, you know, in the straw. It was not clean. And in the winter, it was 50 below 0. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When you went out to work, when you open your mouth, everything got frozen. Everyone's hands was chapped. Blood came out. It was like no skin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What provisions did they make for the cold, the Russians? How did they help you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't. They didn't help nobody. Oh, who worked in the forest, they give you warm clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pants. Tufted pants, you know, like we have the tufted collars here. Pants and coats, jackets. Warmth. And there was-- we had-- the boots was from cloth made, and a big shawl over your head. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that have to do--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was wet at night. We came home, everything was frozen till we warmed them up by the oven.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the summer when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Summer was-- was hot. Summer was good. So we didn't have to have these clothes. We got, you know, like schmattas. You know, I have a rag. Who has a rag? Who had-- everybody said, I give you this. You got this. Give me the piece of bread, I give you a dress. That was the life. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other things happened to people in Siberia when you were there? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Everybody was in pain. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say pain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pain, emotional pain. Everybody said, this is it. We're not going to go out from here. This is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you feel hopeless?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about among yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talk about if someday is going to come-- one evening, it was we were in one place, in the other barracks. And it was young people. And we start to laugh. And we start to say-- I said the first thing, I said, look, if the day's going to come, we're going to sit by the table, and it's going to be bread on the table. And we're going to eat, and it's going to-- and I'm going to say, I have enough. A piece of bread is leftover, I don't think so if it's going to come this day. But it eventually came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of illnesses did people get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of people got malaria. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any epidemics of illnesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
